title: Does the Man Who Accuses Us Have One Tongue or Two?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. The English-language court quotations printed in the letter have been preserved. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. Political and legal allegations remain attributed to the letter and its quoted orders. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR, mechanical word-joining errors, malformed punctuation, and the damaged `14-3-2014` OCR date have been repaired from the page images. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In several letters to my Udanpirappu, I continuously gave you information about the disproportionate-assets case in Bengaluru before judgment was delivered. Since a widespread impression has arisen that I have remained silent and expressed no view after the judgment, and since Special Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh has criticised the DMK in an interview today, I wish to offer some observations about public prosecutors, and Bhavani Singh in particular.
In his interview, Bhavani Singh said:

“How can anyone say I acted in support of the accused? Those who do not know the law may criticise my position. I acted according to my conscience. From the day the Karnataka Government appointed me in accordance with a Supreme Court order, DMK members said I was acting for the accused. I proved that false through the judgment. They repeat the same criticism even now. This is not right.”
Since he has said this, I shall explain his conduct.

When the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and the others was first tried in Tamil Nadu, the AIADMK was in power. The trial failed to proceed properly, and witnesses who had earlier given proper evidence were threatened and made to change their testimony. Our General Secretary, the Professor, therefore petitioned to transfer the case to another State.
On 18 November 2003, a Supreme Court Bench comprising Justices S.N. Variava and H.K. Sema ordered the case transferred to Bengaluru. Do you know what the Judges said?

> “It does appear that the new public prosecutor is hand in glove with the accused thereby creating a reasonable apprehension of likelihood of failure of justice in the minds of the public at large. There is strong indication that the process of justice is being subverted.”

When the accused were to be questioned under Section 313, Jayalalithaa sought exemption from appearing personally. It was regrettable that the Public Prosecutor merely watched without opposing that frivolous application:
> “The conduct of the Public Prosecutor in not opposing such a frivolous application has to be deprecated.”
The Supreme Court strongly objected to the Trial Court exempting Jayalalithaa from personal appearance:

> “Be you ever so high, the law is above you. The grounds cited by her in the application were not at all mitigating circumstances to have granted dispensation of personal appearance. To say the least, that was a ploy adopted to circumvent the due process of law.”

The Court further directed every accused to appear without fail when questioned, ordered that the case be heard daily, and required the Tamil Nadu Government immediately to send every relevant record to the Special Court.

The Supreme Court ordered an expeditious, day-to-day trial on 18 November 2003, when the AIADMK governed Tamil Nadu. Do you know when the Chennai Special Court issued the formal order transferring the case to Bengaluru? On 10 September 2004, ten months later.
That was the position then. Do you know the present position?

A Public Prosecutor must argue against the accused. Bhavani Singh, however, did not do so. In a manner that weakened the prosecution itself, he allowed the Investigating Officer to be examined as a defence witness. He was also eager to conclude final arguments without bringing the seized gold and diamond jewellery under the control of the court trying the case.

On these grounds, our Professor petitioned the Karnataka High Court to remove Bhavani Singh. The Karnataka Government acted immediately and removed him as Public Prosecutor. But Jayalalithaa’s side, the very accused against whom he was required to argue, went up to the Supreme Court demanding that Bhavani Singh continue as prosecutor.

Many examples of conduct favourable to Jayalalithaa’s side and harmful to the prosecution were placed before the Supreme Court. Even though the Karnataka Government had replaced him, Supreme Court Justice Chauhan decided that Bhavani Singh could continue conducting the prosecution.

At the hearing on 27 January 2014, counsel for the accused applied to exempt them from personal appearance. They said Jayalalithaa had official duties as Chief Minister; Sasikala had eye pain; Sudhakaran had joint pain; and Ilavarasi had diabetes and could not attend court.

Bhavani Singh, the opposing counsel whose duty was to object, raised no objection whatever. Because he did not object, the Judge accepted the request, while observing: “The petitions seeking exemption from appearance are not accompanied by proper medical certificates. Nevertheless, I accept them for this occasion alone.”

Jayalalithaa’s counsel then filed a fresh application seeking return of property seized during searches of their homes but said to be unrelated to the case. When the Judge asked the Public Prosecutor to state whether he objected, Bhavani Singh requested two weeks and sought adjournment of the trial.

Expressing dissatisfaction, the Judge asked the crucial question: “Is this a criminal court or an adjournment court?” Special Judge Cunha further observed that the case had continued for fifteen years; that the days of adjournment exceeded the days of hearing; and that the purpose of appointing a Special Court and Special Prosecutor was to hold daily proceedings and conclude the trial quickly. That purpose had not been fully achieved, he said, and at least from then onward the case must proceed without interruption.
At the hearing on 20 January, Jayalalithaa’s counsel had sought every detail and the list of gifts among the movable properties brought from Chennai to Bengaluru. The Judge rejected that application.

The Judge then announced that final arguments would begin on 3 February, that the case would be heard daily under the Supreme Court’s order, that the prosecution would argue first and the defence afterward, and that final arguments could not be delayed under any circumstances.

Even after more than fifteen years of planned delay, various tactics were employed to prolong the case still further. One was a petition filed by Bhavani Singh himself. He asked the Judge to order that 1,116 kilograms of silver articles belonging to Jayalalithaa, which had been handed to one Bhaskaran in Chennai, be brought to the Bengaluru court.

Why did the Public Prosecutor need to make such an application when the case was nearing its end? It was filed with the intention of delaying the case. A petition on behalf of the Professor informed the Court that Bhaskaran had already died.
Rejecting Bhavani Singh’s application on 28 February 2014, the Special Judge said in substance:

The case had proceeded in Bengaluru for more than nine years. When Judge P.A. Mallikarjunaiah heard it, the accused appeared and gave statements under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. When Judge Balakrishna heard it, witness examination was completed and both prosecution and defence concluded their arguments. Judge Balakrishna had recorded as early as 14 August 2013 that every matter in the case had been completed. The case had now reached its final stage.

For thirteen years, the prosecution had taken no step to obtain these silver articles from Bhaskaran and place them before the Court. When, following the Professor’s petition, gold and silver ornaments in Chennai were brought to Bengaluru, why did Bhavani Singh not apply to bring these articles from Bhaskaran?

Bhavani Singh had served as prosecutor since February 2013 and filed no such petition during that period. His claim that he learned of the silver only while reading records for final arguments was neither truthful nor believable. Even after knowing Bhaskaran was dead, he continued to demand the articles from him. Even when the death was proved with a death certificate, Bhavani Singh refused to accept it and persisted with his petition. This was legally unacceptable.

The Judge concluded that Bhavani Singh appeared to have acted with the object of delaying the case; there was no need to waste time; the argument was unsound; the petition had no basis and was unnecessary; and it was therefore dismissed.
When the Special Judge wrote all this about Bhavani Singh’s application, do we need another example to understand how accommodating he had been toward the accused?

Judge John Michael D’Cunha announced that final arguments would begin on 7 March 2014. The Public Prosecutor did not appear that day, and the case was adjourned to the tenth. Bhavani Singh did not appear on the tenth either.

When the case came up on 14 March 2014, the Judge ordered final arguments to begin. Bhavani Singh appeared, claimed ill health, requested ten more days and produced medical certificates. Dissatisfied with those certificates, the Judge ordered Bhavani Singh, who had appeared but refused to argue, to pay a fine equal to his daily fee of Rs. 65,000.

On the fifteenth, Bhavani Singh again failed to appear, without notice or explanation. His assistant Murugesh said he knew nothing about the prosecutor’s absence. The Judge therefore imposed another fine of Rs. 65,000, Bhavani Singh’s fee for that day.
Jayalalithaa’s lawyers said they could begin their arguments only after the prosecutor concluded his. The Judge then firmly ordered Bhavani Singh to begin on 21 March 2014.
Meanwhile, Bhavani Singh petitioned the Karnataka High Court to cancel the Special Court’s fine for his nonappearance. Justice Sathyanarayana Rao dismissed his petition.

The Karnataka Government had removed a prosecutor appointed to argue against Jayalalithaa after allegations that he had failed in his duty. Jayalalithaa’s side nevertheless went to the Supreme Court demanding that the same Bhavani Singh continue, and Justice Chauhan permitted him to do so. He then repeatedly failed to appear in the Special Court, was fined by the Special Judge, appealed to the Karnataka High Court, and had his petition dismissed there too.

Bhavani Singh now beats his chest claiming that his excellent arguments secured punishment for the accused. Yet Judge Cunha recorded in his judgment that “Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh merely read the examination-in-chief of the prosecution witnesses and concluded his arguments.” From this alone, one can understand how he performed in the case.
It is Bhavani Singh, entitled to such “glories,” who now gives an interview accusing the DMK of making false allegations against him.

Even now, when the Karnataka High Court considered bail for the convicted persons, Bhavani Singh filed a forceful written objection in the morning saying bail must not be granted. By evening, he orally recommended that bail could be granted, leaving everyone closely following the proceedings bewildered. What happened between what he submitted in writing that morning and what he said orally that evening?
It is not the DMK that comments upon him. Do you know what Karnataka High Court Justice A.V. Chandrashekara, who heard the bail application, wrote?

The Judge noted that Special Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh, who had represented the State in the disproportionate-assets case and obtained imprisonment and fines against the accused, stated in his written objections filed on the thirtieth: “If the accused are released on bail, they will abscond, creating difficulty in executing the Court’s sentence.” Yet the same lawyer later changed his position and said: “The accused may be released on conditional bail.”
The Judge ruled that even if the Government wished to release them, the nature of their offences meant that they had lost the moral right to bail, and the Court would not grant it.
Was this observation in Justice A.V. Chandrashekara’s order a certificate of appreciation for Bhavani Singh? Will Bhavani Singh answer?
With affection,
M.K.
11 October 2014
